result:
:::writing
The night the clocks forgot themselves, nobody noticed at first.

It was a small thing, almost polite. Seconds stretched, then folded. A minute lingered a little too long over a cup of cooling tea, then slipped away before anyone could complain. On Maple Street, the bakery’s wall clock ticked with a soft, obedient rhythm, but the hands circled a little slower than the dough rose. In the hospital across town, a monitor counted heartbeats with perfect seriousness, unaware that somewhere between one beat and the next, an extra breath had been tucked in like a secret.

Only Mara felt it right away.

She was halfway through locking the door of the antique shop when the key hesitated, as if it had forgotten what it was supposed to do. Not jammed—just…uncertain. Mara held still, listening. The street hummed as usual: a passing car, a distant laugh, the whisper of wind through the sycamores. And underneath it, something else. A pause that didn’t belong.

She turned the key again. It clicked into place with a decisive, almost embarrassed sound.

“Get a grip,” she murmured, though she wasn’t sure whether she was speaking to the lock or to herself.

Inside the shop, clocks lined the walls like quiet witnesses. Tall grandfather clocks with solemn faces. Brass carriage clocks with tiny, meticulous gears. A chipped plastic alarm clock from the 80s that had outlived three owners and a brief stint as a doorstop. Mara had spent years repairing them, coaxing stubborn mechanisms back into rhythm. Time, she often said, was less a river and more a negotiation.

Tonight, the negotiation had gone strange.

She flicked on the lights. A dozen second hands leapt forward in unison, then—just for a blink—hesitated.

Mara froze.

There it was again. Not a sound, not exactly. More like the absence of a sound that should have been there. The space between ticks stretched, as if someone had gently pulled the world like taffy.

“Okay,” she said, louder now. “That’s new.”

She moved to the nearest clock, a walnut grandfather she’d been restoring for weeks. Its pendulum swung with dignified patience. Tick. Tock. Tick…

The next tock came late.

Mara leaned in, her ear close to the case. The wood smelled faintly of varnish and something older, something like memory. She counted under her breath.

“One…two…three…”

Tick.

But she was certain there had been four.

She straightened slowly, a prickle creeping up her arms. “You’re all doing it, aren’t you?” she said to the room.

The clocks, naturally, did not answer. But their silence felt crowded, as if it had opinions.

Mara grabbed her phone. 10:17 PM. She watched the numbers. The minute held steady. 10:17.

And held.

And held.

Then, without warning, it flipped to 10:19.

She blinked. “That’s not how that works.”

The shop seemed to lean in around her, every polished surface reflecting her own confusion back at her. For a moment, she considered the obvious explanations—faulty phone, power surge, some citywide glitch. But the feeling in the air didn’t match anything so mundane. It felt…intentional.

As if time itself had taken a breath and forgotten to let it out.

A soft chime rang from the back room.

Mara turned. She hadn’t set any timers. The chime came again, delicate and insistent. It was coming from the old clock she’d refused to sell.

It stood alone on a small table, half-hidden beneath a dust cloth. No markings, no brand. Just a simple circular face and a single hand. It didn’t tell hours or minutes—only something else, something Mara had never quite figured out.

She approached it slowly. “You again,” she said.

The clock chimed once more.

Mara pulled off the cloth.

The hand was moving.

That, in itself, wasn’t unusual. What was unusual was the way it moved—smoothly, continuously, like a breath drawn out forever. No ticks. No increments. Just motion.

“Okay,” Mara said, her voice steadier than she felt. “If this is your idea of a joke—”

The hand stopped.

Not gradually. Not with the gentle deceleration of a winding mechanism. It simply…ceased.

And in that instant, the world followed.

The hum of the lights vanished. The distant car froze mid-pass. The wind outside held its breath in the branches. Even Mara’s own heartbeat seemed to pause, suspended in a fragile, impossible stillness.

She stood there, not daring to move.

Then, very carefully, she inhaled.

The air felt thick, like stepping into water. Her chest resisted, then yielded. The breath came in, slow and deliberate, as if it had to remember how.

“You stopped it,” she whispered to the clock.

The clock, being a clock, did not respond.

Mara reached out. Her fingers hovered just above the glass. “If I touch you,” she said, “does everything start again?”

No answer.

She laughed softly, the sound oddly muted in the frozen room. “Of course you don’t answer. That would be too easy.”

She thought of all the clocks she’d fixed over the years. How each one had its own stubbornness, its own peculiar logic. You couldn’t force them. You had to listen. Adjust. Wait.

Time, she’d always believed, was something you partnered with.

“Well,” she said, “I’m listening.”

Nothing happened.

She frowned. “Right. Helpful.”

Mara closed her eyes.

What did time feel like, when it was working? Not the numbers on a screen, not the ticking of a mechanism—but the actual experience of it. The way a moment could stretch when you were afraid, or collapse when you were happy. The way a second could be heavy or light, depending on what it carried.

She focused on her breath. In. Out. Or…in, at least. The out was still waiting somewhere.

“This isn’t broken,” she murmured. “It’s…stuck.”

Her eyes opened. She looked at the single hand on the clock, frozen mid-sweep.

“Where were you going?” she asked it.

A ridiculous question.

But the kind you ask when logic has already left the room.

Mara tilted her head, studying the angle of the hand. It pointed somewhere between two invisible markers. Not quite here. Not quite there.

“In between,” she said.

The word seemed to settle into the silence, as if it belonged.

“In between what?”

She glanced around the shop. Every clock was caught in its own private almost. A second not finished. A minute not begun.

“Everything’s…waiting,” she realized.

For what?

Her gaze returned to the clock. Slowly, she lowered her finger until it touched the glass.

The world did not explode. Time did not surge forward.

Instead, something much smaller happened.

The hand moved.

Just a fraction. Barely enough to notice.

But it was enough.

A distant car completed its pass. The wind exhaled through the trees. Somewhere, a clock ticked—once.

Mara’s heart slammed into motion, finishing the beat it had started.

She sucked in a full breath, then let it out in a rush. “Okay,” she said, a little breathless. “Okay, I see you.”

The hand had stopped again, but now it felt…poised. Like a held note that wanted to resolve.

“You don’t need to be pushed,” Mara said slowly. “You just need permission.”

She rested her palm flat against the glass. “Go on.”

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the hand began to move.

Not smoothly, not yet—but with a tentative, gathering confidence. It crept forward, then a little faster, then faster still, until it was sweeping in a steady arc.

And with it, the world resumed.

The lights hummed back to life. The clocks around the shop burst into their various rhythms, ticks overlapping in a chaotic, comforting chorus. Mara’s phone buzzed in her pocket, suddenly very eager to catch up.

She laughed, the sound bright and relieved. “There you are.”

The strange clock’s hand slowed, then settled into a gentle, continuous motion.

Mara stepped back, wiping her hands on her jeans. “Next time,” she said, “maybe a warning?”

The clock, as expected, offered none.

But the air felt right again. The pauses were where they belonged—small, ordinary, unremarkable.

Mara glanced at her phone. 10:18 PM.

She stared at it, then snorted. “Close enough.”

As she turned off the lights and locked up for the night, the key slid smoothly into place, no hesitation this time.

Out on Maple Street, life carried on, blissfully unaware that it had nearly been caught between moments.

Mara pulled her jacket tighter and started home, the steady rhythm of her footsteps syncing with the quiet, dependable ticking of the world.

For now, at least, time remembered how to move.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
